name: Dal Lake
excerpt: The jewel of Srinagar — shikara rides at dawn, floating gardens, and houseboats moored along the boulevard.
description: Dal Lake spans roughly 18 sq km and has been the centre of Srinagar life for centuries. Houseboats here date back to British-era regulations; many families have run the same boat for three generations. Shikara rides are best booked early — before 8 AM when the vegetable market sets up on the water.

name: Gulmarg
excerpt: Meadows at 2,650 m, Asia's highest gondola, and powder slopes that draw skiers from across the country.
description: Gulmarg means "meadow of flowers" — apt in summer when wildflowers cover the bowl-shaped valley. The Gulmarg Gondola (Phase 1 & 2) takes you to Kongdoori and then Apharwat Peak. Winter months see heavy snowfall; check road status on the J&K Tourism helpline before driving up from Tangmarg.

name: Pahalgam
excerpt: Where the Lidder River cuts through pine forests — base camp for the Amarnath Yatra and quiet meadow walks.
description: Pahalgam sits at the confluence of streams from Sheshnag Lake and Lidder River. Betaab Valley and Aru are short drives away. The town gets crowded during Amarnath Yatra season (July); plan accommodation well in advance if visiting then.

name: Sonamarg
excerpt: Meadow of gold at the foot of the Zoji La — gateway to Ladakh and Thajiwas Glacier day hikes.
description: Sonamarg is a day-trip favourite from Srinagar (about 80 km). Thajiwas Glacier is reachable by pony or a steep walk — go with a local guide if unfamiliar with altitude. The Srinagar–Leh highway passes through here; landslides can block the road after heavy rain.

name: Mughal Gardens
excerpt: Terraced chinar-lined gardens from the 16th century — Shalimar, Nishat, and Chashme Shahi on the Zabarwan hills.
description: Built during Jahangir and Shah Jahan's reigns, these gardens follow the Persian charbagh layout. Nishat Bagh has twelve terraces representing the zodiac; Shalimar was the royal leisure garden. Combined ticket available at the Tourism counter near Dal Gate.

name: Dachigam National Park
excerpt: Home of the endangered Hangul (Kashmir stag) — dense deodar forest 22 km from the city centre.
description: Dachigam was originally a royal hunting preserve. Today it protects the Hangul, black bear, and leopard (rarely seen). Upper Dachigam requires a separate permit and is usually open May–August. Lower Dachigam is more accessible for day visits.
